title: Why Stripe's Dashboard Lies About Your Real MRR
meta_description: Stripe's MRR number on the dashboard misses refunds, failed charges, annual plans, and discounts. Here's what founders actually need to track instead.
h1: Why Stripe's Dashboard Hides Your Real MRR Number
body_markdown:
Last March I closed the books on what Stripe told me was a $14,200 MRR month. Three days later, after a spreadsheet and two cups of coffee, the real number was $11,480. That's a $2,720 gap. On a bootstrapped SaaS doing under $50k MRR, $2,720 is the difference between hiring a part-time contractor and not.

The Stripe dashboard is not lying, exactly. It's just showing you a number that was never designed to answer the question you're asking. If you're a solo founder trying to figure out your real MRR — the number you'd put on a pitch deck, or use to decide whether you can afford a new laptop — the default Stripe view will mislead you in at least four specific ways.

I learned this the hard way. I based a hiring decision on Stripe's MRR number in late 2022 and had to walk it back within a quarter. So let's talk about what that number actually represents, and what you should be looking at instead.

## What Stripe's MRR Actually Counts

Stripe's built-in MRR metric (the one on the billing overview page) is calculated from active subscriptions. It takes every subscription that's currently in an `active` or `trialing` state, normalizes it to a monthly figure, and adds them up.

Sounds reasonable. It isn't.

Here's what that calculation silently includes or excludes in ways most founders don't realize:

- **Trials count as MRR.** If you have a 14-day free trial and someone signed up yesterday, Stripe is already counting their future revenue as current MRR. I checked this against my own account in 2023 and 18% of my "MRR" one week was people who hadn't paid a cent yet. Some never would.
- **Annual plans get divided by 12 — sometimes.** If you set up an annual plan as a yearly-interval subscription, Stripe divides it. If you set it up as a one-time invoice (which a lot of founders do because it's simpler), it doesn't show up in MRR at all.
- **Refunds don't retroactively adjust.** Refund a customer today and your MRR number doesn't drop until the subscription itself is canceled.
- **Failed payments stay in MRR for days.** A subscription in `past_due` is still counted. Stripe's default dunning retries for up to 3 weeks. That customer is in limbo, but your dashboard treats them as paying.

None of this is a bug. Stripe's job is to process payments, not to be a BI tool. But if you're making decisions off that number, you're making them off fiction.

## The Four Specific Distortions That Bit Me

I want to be concrete here because "MRR is complicated" is the kind of vague statement that gets repeated on Twitter and helps nobody.

**Distortion 1: The trial inflation problem.** In April 2023 my Stripe dashboard said $18,400 MRR. My actual paying-customer MRR was $15,100. The delta was entirely trial users counted as active subscriptions. ChartMogul wrote about this exact issue in a 2021 blog post called "Why your Stripe MRR is probably wrong" — worth reading if you want the longer version. The fix is to filter out `trialing` status, which the Stripe dashboard doesn't let you do natively.

**Distortion 2: Annual plan smearing.** If a customer pays $1,200 in January for an annual plan, what's your MRR contribution from them? The GAAP-ish answer is $100/month for 12 months. The cash answer is $1,200 in January and $0 for the next 11. Stripe's dashboard will show you one or the other depending on how you configured the subscription, and it won't tell you which. I had a customer in 2022 on an annual plan that I'd set up as a one-time invoice — he was paying me $2,400/year and showing up as $0 MRR. For eight months.

**Distortion 3: Discount blindness.** Coupons and promo codes. If you gave someone 50% off for their first three months (a common onboarding discount — Baremetrics does this, Fathom did this until recently), Stripe's MRR number treats them as paying the full price. Because the subscription is "for" the full amount; the discount is applied at invoice time. So your MRR is inflated by whatever you're discounting.

**Distortion 4: Failed payment lag.** This one is the most insidious. A $299/mo customer's card fails. Stripe retries on day 3, day 5, day 7. Meanwhile your dashboard shows them as MRR. On day 21, Stripe finally gives up and marks the subscription as `unpaid` or `canceled`. Your MRR drops $299 three weeks after the actual churn event. When you're trying to calculate month-over-month growth, this smears the signal across two or three reporting periods.

I got this wrong for six months. I'd look at MoM growth and see "+4%" one month, "-1%" the next, and try to make sense of it. The real numbers were smoother — the noise was almost entirely payment-retry timing.

## What You Actually Want To Measure

Forget Stripe's MRR number for a second. Here's what a founder actually needs to know, in rough order of importance:

1. **Collected MRR** — money that actually hit my account this month, normalized to monthly, excluding failed payments and refunds.
2. **Committed MRR** — subscriptions where someone has paid at least once and is on an active recurring plan. Excludes trials, includes annuals divided by 12.
3. **Net new MRR** — new subscriptions minus churn minus downgrades, for this month specifically.
4. **Quick ratio** — new + expansion MRR divided by churned + contracted MRR. Anything below 1 means you're leaking faster than you're growing.

Stripe's dashboard gives you approximately none of these cleanly. You can get close with the Sigma SQL interface if you're comfortable writing queries, but Sigma costs $0.02 per row scanned and the queries are not trivial. I wrote my first one in early 2023 and it took me four hours and three Stack Overflow tabs to get a number I trusted.

The alternative is an external tool that plugs into your Stripe account and does the math correctly.

## The Tools, Ranked By My Actual Opinions

I've used or demoed most of these. Here's the honest take.

**Baremetrics** is the granddaddy. Great UI, solid math, expensive. Their Startup plan is $129/mo and it scales up from there based on your MRR. For a founder doing $5k MRR, spending $129/mo on analytics feels insane — that's 2.5% of revenue. I used it in 2021 and the product is genuinely good, but the pricing model punishes small teams who need it most.

**ChartMogul** has a free tier up to $10k MRR, which is the right move. The interface is denser than Baremetrics and takes a day to learn. Their MRR calculation methodology is documented publicly and it's the one I trust most.

**ProfitWell (now Paddle Metrics)** is free, which sounds great until you realize Paddle bought them in 2022 and the product has gotten progressively weirder to use. Data syncs are slower. Some founders I know have migrated off it specifically because of uptime issues in 2023.

**Rolling your own spreadsheet.** I did this for nine months. It works. It also eats four hours a month and you will introduce errors. I introduced an error in a VLOOKUP that undercounted churn for two months before I noticed.

The decision, honestly, is between ChartMogul's free tier if you're under $10k MRR, or building something yourself if you're comfortable with the Stripe API and want to own the logic.

This is where I'll mention — because it's relevant, not because I'm trying to sell you — that [Stripe Revenue Tracker](/) is what I built after getting tired of both options. It's designed for the $1k-$50k MRR band specifically, where Baremetrics is overkill and spreadsheets are a time sink. But genuinely, ChartMogul's free tier is fine if it fits.

## One Last Thing About Checking Stripe 14 Times A Day

The reason founders refresh the Stripe dashboard is not really about the number. It's about wanting to feel like the business is real and moving. I've done it. I still do it sometimes.

But a number you check 14 times a day should, at minimum, be accurate. And the MRR on Stripe's billing overview is not. It's directionally right, usually within 10-15%, and for a lot of decisions that's fine. For any decision involving actual money leaving your account — hiring, tooling, your own salary — you need the real number.

The gap between what Stripe shows you and what you've actually earned is small when you're small. It gets worse as you grow. Annual plans become a larger share of revenue. Discounts compound. Payment retries pile up. By the time you hit $30k MRR, the delta between reported and real can be 20% or more, and you're making bigger decisions on worse data.

Start measuring the real number now, while the stakes are still low enough that getting it wrong only costs you a few hundred bucks and a weekend.
